components.role: OTA (Operational Transconductance Amplifier) — voltage-controlled gain element; gain is proportional to bias current
Envelope detector op-amp — rectifies and smooths the input signal to derive a control voltage
Output buffer — provides low-impedance output from OTA current output
Envelope rectifier — half-wave rectifier for envelope detection
Envelope rectifier — second diode for full-wave rectification (optional)
Envelope input resistor — scales signal into detector
Envelope smoothing capacitor — sets the overall envelope response time
Attack time resistor — sets how fast the compressor responds to transients
Release time resistor — sets how fast the compressor recovers after signal drops
Sustain/compression pot — controls the compression ratio by scaling the control voltage
Output level pot — compensates for gain reduction, sets output volume
OTA output load resistor — converts OTA output current to voltage

dsp_model.description: Envelope follower driving variable-gain element; envelope controls gain reduction in real time

behavioral_description: An OTA-based compressor that uses the LM13700's voltage-controlled transconductance to smoothly vary the signal gain based on an envelope-detected control voltage. The OTA naturally produces a clean, transparent gain change with minimal distortion artifacts. The attack control determines how quickly the compressor clamps down on transients — fast attack (1-5ms) squashes pick attack for a smooth, even sustain; slow attack (20-50ms) lets the initial transient through, preserving percussive snap. The release controls the recovery — fast release can cause audible 'pumping' and 'breathing' effects (sometimes desirable), while slow release gives a more natural decay. This is the topology behind the Ross Compressor, Dyna Comp, and many studio compressors. The OTA introduces a subtle, pleasant coloration that many guitarists prefer over VCA or optical compressors.
